Idiomatic Expressions

  • 咦！假即真，真即假 - Exclamatory phrase meaning 'Ah! False becomes true, true becomes false,' expressing a paradoxical or ironic statement.
  • 我与我兮，是耶非耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'Am I myself or am I not myself?' reflecting a state of confusion or duality.
  • 顿灭幻躯 - Means 'suddenly extinguish the illusory body,' referring to the disappearance of a physical form that is not real.
  • 衣衫如蜕 - Metaphor meaning 'clothes shed like a snake's skin,' describing the sudden change or disappearance of clothing.
  • 大哗 - Means 'great commotion' or 'uproar,' indicating a loud and chaotic reaction.
  • 不能穷其变 - Means 'unable to exhaust its changes,' suggesting that the transformation is too complex or mysterious to fully understand.
  • 先与女闲话之马二娘 - Refers to a woman named '马二娘' who was previously engaged in casual conversation with the woman in question.
  • 鸨而猾者也 - Describes '马二娘' as a 'prostitute and cunning one,' indicating her profession and shrewdness.
  • 瞰女美 - Means 'seeing the woman as beautiful,' describing her physical attractiveness.
  • 欲攘为钱树子 - Means 'wanting to seize her as a money tree,' metaphorically referring to exploiting her for financial gain.
  • 以贾妻毒焰告女 - Means 'telling the woman about the jealous rage of her husband,' indicating that the husband is possessive or violent.
  • 不信 - Means 'does not believe,' showing the woman's skepticism.
  • 蹈隙恐贾 - Means 'taking advantage of an opportunity to frighten her husband,' describing a strategic move to manipulate the situation.
  • 秀才祸不远矣 - Means 'the scholar's misfortune is not far off,' predicting impending trouble for the scholar.
  • 君家胭脂虎 - Derogatory term meaning 'your household's lipstick tiger,' referring to a jealous or possessive woman.
  • 砸碎醋坛子 - Metaphor meaning 'breaking the vinegar jar,' symbolizing the end of jealousy or possessiveness.
  • 懵懵耶 - Means 'isn't he clueless?' expressing disbelief or mockery at the scholar's lack of awareness.
  • 鸨冷笑云 - Means 'the prostitute coldly says,' describing her dismissive attitude.
  • 东邻卖货者来 - Means 'a neighbor selling goods comes,' referring to a passerby or visitor.
  • 阃人知汝买艳妇 - Means 'the woman in the inner quarters knows you bought a beautiful woman,' indicating gossip or information from within the household.
  • 骂不绝声云 - Means 'continuously scolding and saying,' describing persistent verbal abuse.
  • 俟红妆到门 - Means 'awaiting the arrival of the bride,' referring to the anticipated arrival of the woman.
  • 当以白刃毕命 - Means 'will use a white blade to end her life,' threatening violence or murder.
  • 嗒然求计于鸨 - Means 'helplessly seeking advice from the prostitute,' showing the scholar's desperation.
  • 是何难哉 - Means 'what is difficult about this?' expressing a sense of ease or simplicity.
  • 姑以女与我 - Means 'let me have the woman,' suggesting a demand or request.
  • 汝只身归 - Means 'you return alone,' indicating that the scholar will be left without the woman.
  • 是非犹可狡辩 - Means 'right and wrong can still be argued cleverly,' suggesting that the scholar can still defend himself.
  • 譬诸盗无赃物也 - Means 'it's like a thief with no stolen goods,' implying that the scholar can avoid blame or punishment.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 假即真，真即假 - Reflects the philosophical concept of the interplay between reality and illusion, common in Chinese thought.
  • 鸨而猾者也 - Refers to the historical and cultural role of prostitutes in Chinese society, often depicted as cunning and manipulative.
  • 胭脂虎 - A term historically used to describe a jealous or possessive wife, reflecting societal attitudes toward marital relationships.
  • 醋坛子 - A metaphor for jealousy, derived from the idea that jealousy is like the sourness of vinegar.
  • 阃人 - Refers to the inner quarters of a household, traditionally the domain of women, reflecting the gendered division of space in Chinese culture.
  • 红妆 - Refers to a woman dressed in red, symbolizing marriage or the arrival of a bride in Chinese tradition.
  • 白刃 - Refers to a sword or knife, symbolizing violence or death in Chinese literature.

  • 胭脂虎 - A derogatory term for the scholar's wife, a proper noun that must be consistently rendered.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 旋出百金授贾 - 旋 means 'immediately' or 'right away,' and 出百金 means 'gave out a hundred gold coins.' This phrase indicates an immediate and significant action.
  • 尚不丰耶 - 尚不丰耶 means 'is it not enough?' This is a rhetorical question emphasizing the perceived inadequacy.
  • 贾惑之，乃囊金弃女，如飞去 - 贾惑之 means 'was deceived by it,' 囊金 means 'put the money in a bag,' 弃女 means 'abandoned the girl,' and 如飞去 means 'fled away like a bird.' This describes the merchant's immediate and dramatic reaction.
  • 始爱若明珠 - 始 means 'then' or 'at first,' 爱 means 'loved,' and 若明珠 means 'like a precious pearl.' This emphasizes the sudden change in affection.
  • 旋使应客，不允，詈且挞 - 旋 means 'immediately,' 使应客 means 'made her entertain guests,' 不允 means 'did not agree,' 詈 means 'scolded,' and 挞 means 'beaten.' This describes the harsh treatment.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '旋出百金授贾，曰：“以此买瘦马，尚不丰耶？”' is a direct and immediate action followed by a rhetorical question, emphasizing the merchant's greed and the girl's perceived inadequacy.
  • The sentence '贾惑之，乃囊金弃女，如飞去' describes a series of actions in rapid succession, highlighting the merchant's immediate and dramatic reaction.
  • The sentence '始爱若明珠，旋使应客，不允，詈且挞' describes a sudden change in affection followed by harsh treatment, emphasizing the girl's mistreatment.
  • The sentence '仍倔强，即倒悬，出利刃割其臂肉饲猫犬' describes a series of increasingly cruel punishments, emphasizing the girl's suffering.
  • The sentence '女痛极，佯允之，是日裹创盛妆饰' describes the girl's desperate attempt to avoid further harm followed by her preparation for the encounter.
  • The sentence '适一贵公子来，欣然愿荐枕席' describes the nobleman's offer, emphasizing his intentions.
  • The sentence '漏三下，公子醉而假寐' describes the time and the nobleman's state, setting the scene for the tragic event.
  • The sentence '女闭户卸妆，久无声' describes the girl's actions and the ensuing silence, building tension.
  • The sentence '灯小如豆，公子犹扯女衣作昵声' describes the nobleman's continued advances, emphasizing the girl's vulnerability.
  • The sentence '讵僵立不动；烛之，舌出三寸，已雉经榻前短柱上矣' describes the girl's tragic end, emphasizing the suddenness and finality of her death.
  • The sentence '大号，鸨来救，莫及' describes the madam's futile attempt to save the girl, emphasizing the inevitability of her fate.
  • The sentence '是即女归叩门之夜也' identifies the specific night when the girl returned, providing context for the events.

  • 问：“何不报贾之计赚，与鸨之毒凌？” - The man asks why she did not seek revenge on the man who tricked her and the brothel owner who mistreated her.

  • 向晨视之，树杪一大鸟，人首五色，羽大如车盖，巨爪搏死蛇食，堕者骨也 - Describes seeing a large bird with a human-like head, colorful feathers as large as a carriage canopy, large claws grasping a dead snake to eat, and the fallen objects being bones.
  • 大惧，潜灌药发火，伺其飞起，击之，中胸际，声烈烈，啼如鬼车，展翅生大风，飞空际多时，堕则毙 - Describes the father and son's reaction to the large bird, secretly pouring medicine and lighting a fire, waiting for it to fly up, striking it, hitting its chest, making a loud noise, crying like a ghostly carriage, spreading its wings and creating a strong wind, flying high for a long time, and then falling and dying.
  • 父子大喜，负之归献，边将惊且诧，慰劳甚殷，嘱勿往 - Describes the father and son being greatly happy, carrying the large bird back to present it, the border general being surprised and amazed, giving generous comfort and rewards, and advising not to go there again.

  • 视前象立谷口，若拱候，试登之，果负而趋 - Describes seeing the previous elephant standing at the entrance of the valley, like bowing in greeting, trying to climb onto it, and it indeed carrying and moving forward.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 曰 - Common Classical Chinese word for 'said' or 'spoke'.
  • 奈何 - Phrase meaning 'what to do' or 'how to handle'.
  • 曷 - Archaic interrogative particle meaning 'why' or 'how'.
  • 另作生涯 - Literally 'start a new career', implying a change in circumstances.
  • 此计之善者 - Means 'this is a good plan' or 'this is a wise course of action'.
  • 私议久之 - Means 'after long private discussion', emphasizing the deliberation.
  • 以为然 - Means 'they thought it was right' or 'they agreed'.
  • 问其值 - Means 'ask about its value' or 'inquire about its price'.
  • 如数交兑 - Means 'exchange the full amount' or 'pay the exact sum'.
  • 生命仆人放之去 - Literally 'let the living servant go', but the meaning is unclear without context.
  • 索在颈 - Means 'the rope is around the neck', implying a threat of death.
  • 尚噬人 - Means 'it still bites people', likely referring to the tiger's ferocity.
  • 若解去 - Means 'if you remove it', referring to the rope.
  • 不将继叟伥耶 - Means 'won't you become the next 伥 (a follower of a tiger)?' This is a reference to a folk tale where people who follow tigers become their companions.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 司空 - A high-ranking official title in ancient China, often associated with the Ministry of Works or the Ministry of Public Works.
  • 红丝牵引 - A metaphor for fate or destiny, often used in the context of marriage. It is derived from the legend of the 'Red Thread of Fate,' where a red thread binds the destinies of two people who are meant to be together.
  • 文星 - A star associated with literature and culture, often used to symbolize a person's literary talent or cultural significance.
  • 石证三生 - A stone that serves as proof of three lifetimes, symbolizing a predestined relationship. This concept is rooted in Buddhist beliefs about reincarnation and karma.
  • 箕帚 - A humble reference to a wife's role, derived from the practice of treating a wife like a broom and dustpan, which were used to clean the house.
  • 蓬门 - A humble home, often used to describe a modest or simple dwelling.
  • 瀛第 - A grand or luxurious residence, often used to describe a noble or wealthy home.
  • 朱提 - A type of gold coin used in ancient China, often used as a unit of currency.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 旋即笙管呕哑，灯火匝地，干仆引生之曲室，更簇新冠带，出就氍毹 - This sequence describes a series of actions leading up to the main event, which must be read as a continuous narrative.
  • 雏姬三四，引一二八好女子，珠翠绮罗，盈盈自内出，与生交拜，送之洞房 - This describes the introduction of the main female characters and their interaction with the protagonist.
  • 却扇视女，则荷露桃霞，无比艳冶 - This describes the moment when the protagonist sees his new wife for the first time.
  • 生心意飞驰，反恨顷言新婚暂别，未免孟浪，容有意迁延，图静好耳 - This expresses the protagonist's conflicting emotions and thoughts.
  • 酒阑灯，听莲漏三催，婢妾亡去 - This describes the end of the evening and the departure of the maids.
  • 生正隐几枨触，而女亦时牵绣窥良人，粉黛间隐有惨悴色 - This describes the emotional state of both the protagonist and his wife.
  • 生不知就里，趋近软语，代为卸妆，女则拒以纤腕，再近则潜然流珠泪，徐起弹烛，视近闼无一人，始闭门小语曰 - This describes the protagonist's attempt to comfort his wife and her initial resistance followed by her emotional breakdown.

  • 乞归署木主 - Traditional practice in Chinese funerary rites: 'I ask to be buried with a wooden tablet inscribed with my name.'
  • 结发元配邱氏丽玉之位 - Denotes the deceased's status in the family: 'the position of my original wife, Lady Li Yu.'
  • 瞑目泉台下矣 - Euphemistic way of saying 'I will die': 'I will close my eyes and rest in the underworld.'
  • 曷饮鸩同死，结来生缘乎 - Dramatic expression of a desire for mutual suicide: 'why not drink poison together and die, thus tying our fate for the next life?'
  • 不可，请书居址门巷，与妾纫衣缝中 - Request to ensure the deceased's spirit can find its way back: 'no, please write down my address and street, and place it in the seam of my clothes.'
  • 俾他日柔魂，度关山，省舅姑，受郎君一盂麦饭耳 - Wish for the deceased's spirit to continue its existence in a meaningful way: 'so that one day my gentle spirit can cross mountains and pass through gates, visit my in-laws, and receive a bowl of millet porridge from my husband.'
  • 入衾共枕 - Conveys a sense of closeness and intimacy: 'we got into bed together.'
  • 屡屡不能自持 - Indicates a state of emotional distress: 'I could not control myself repeatedly.'
  • 悉劝慰禁止 - Shows the deceased's efforts to calm the narrator: 'she comforted and restrained me.'
  • 对食不餐 - Metaphorical way of saying they were unable to enjoy their meal due to emotional distress: 'we ate but did not consume anything.'
  • 几与石女天阉，同一恨事 - Metaphorical expression of sexual frustration: 'it was almost like being a stone maiden or a eunuch, the same kind of regret.'
  • 翌日，翁媪果顿同陌路 - Indicates a sudden and dramatic change in their relationship: 'the next day, my parents-in-law indeed became strangers to me.'
  • 女以香舌吮生颈，作胭脂色者三四处 - Vivid description of a physical act: 'the woman used her sweet tongue to lick the back of my neck, leaving red marks in three or four places.'
  • 可矣 - Indicates that the physical act has reached a satisfactory point: 'it is enough.'
  • 私赠黄金白玉缠臂各二 - Gift of valuable items: 'she secretly gave me two bracelets, one of gold and one of jade.'
  • 订后约 - Refers to a promise to meet again: 'to make a future appointment.'
  • 恐君再来，妾墓门之木拱矣 - Metaphorical way of saying the narrator will be dead for a long time: 'I fear that by the time you come again, the trees at my tomb will be huge.'
  • 挥手令去 - Final farewell: 'she waved me away.'

  • 妾虽麻疯得郎生，郎转麻疯为妾死 - '虽' (even though) introduces a concessive clause, '得郎生' (gained life through you) and '转麻疯为妾死' (transformed leprosy into my death) are idiomatic expressions emphasizing the transformation of fate.
  • 郎为妾死郎不知，洞房绣阁衔金卮 - '郎不知' (you do not know) and '洞房绣阁' (embroidered bridal chamber) are idiomatic expressions highlighting the obliviousness of the husband and the luxurious setting.

  • 妾虽生，妾不愿，守故居；妾既生，妾自当，寻我夫 - '妾虽生，妾不愿' (I am alive, but I do not wish) and '妾既生，妾自当' (I am alive, I must) are idiomatic expressions expressing the speaker's conflicting desires.

  • 遣村妇伺应 - Phrase meaning 'sent village women to attend to her' or 'sent village women to serve her'.
  • 妇皆唾却走 - Phrase meaning 'the women all spit out and ran away' or 'the women all rejected and fled'.
  • 幸老尼怜悯 - Phrase meaning 'fortunately, the old nun had compassion' or 'fortunately, the old nun pitied her'.
  • 得无苦 - Phrase meaning 'was able to avoid suffering' or 'was able to live without suffering'.
  • 月余 - Phrase meaning 'more than a month' or 'a month or so'.
  • 生归 - Phrase meaning 'the protagonist returned' or 'the protagonist came back'.
  • 生惊惶不知所云 - Phrase meaning 'the protagonist was frightened and did not know what was being said' or 'the protagonist was alarmed and did not understand what was being said'.
  • 是不可负也 - Phrase meaning 'this cannot be betrayed' or 'this cannot be broken'.
  • 吾家不少闲粥饭 - Phrase meaning 'my family does not lack idle food' or 'my family does not lack unearned meals'.
  • 虽易枕席，当豢之，终其身 - Phrase meaning 'even if we change beds, we should feed him for his entire life' or 'even if we change beds, we should take care of him for his entire life'.
  • 生伏谢 - Phrase meaning 'the protagonist bowed and thanked' or 'the protagonist prostrated and thanked'.
  • 急趋访女 - Phrase meaning 'hurriedly went to visit the woman' or 'quickly went to see the woman'.
  • 遽牵生衣啼曰 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly pulled his clothes and cried, saying' or 'immediately grabbed his clothes and wept, saying'.
  • 妾远来，不敢望伉俪，唯冀以骸骨葬君家祖域耳 - Phrase meaning 'I came from afar, do not dare to hope for a marital union, only hope to be buried in your family's ancestral grounds' or 'I came from afar, do not dare to expect a marriage, only hope to be buried in your family's ancestral grounds'.
  • 生且泣且慰 - Phrase meaning 'the protagonist cried and comforted' or 'the protagonist wept and consoled'.
  • 问何能自来 - Phrase meaning 'asked how she could come here' or 'inquired how she could arrive here'.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 心憶蛇毒或可代鸩 - Metaphorical expression suggesting that the venom of a snake might substitute for the poison of a sparrow hawk (鸩), implying a comparison of potency or effect.
  • 掬饮升许 - "掬" means to scoop up with the hands, and "升许" refers to a small quantity, possibly a unit of measurement.
  • 心顿清醒 - "顿" indicates a sudden change, so this phrase means "suddenly became clear-headed."
  • 怯烦襟 - "怯" can mean to feel timid or uneasy, and "烦襟" refers to a troubled or anxious heart. Together, it suggests a sense of relief from anxiety.
  • 肤转奇痒 - "转" means to change or become, and "奇痒" refers to an unusual or intense itch. This phrase indicates that the skin suddenly became very itchy.
  • 又掬以洗涤 - "又" means "again," and "洗涤" means to wash or cleanse. This phrase suggests repeating the act of washing.
  • 痒顿止 - "顿止" means "suddenly stopped," indicating that the itching ceased abruptly.
  • 明日，又潜饮而潜洗之 - "明日" means "the next day," and "潜" means "secretly" or "in secret." This phrase suggests that the action was repeated in a hidden manner.
  • 疾若失 - "若失" means "as if lost," suggesting that the disease vanished suddenly.
  • 肤之燥者，转莹如玉 - "燥者" means "dry," and "莹如玉" means "shining like jade." This phrase describes the skin becoming smooth and radiant.
  • 发之卷者，转垂若云 - "卷者" means "curly," and "垂若云" means "drooping like clouds." This phrase describes the hair becoming straight and flowing.
  • 面目手足之皴者，转如花如月，如嫩笋芽矣 - "皴者" means "wrinkled," and "如花如月，如嫩笋芽" means "like flowers, like the moon, like tender bamboo shoots." This phrase describes the face, hands, and feet becoming smooth and youthful.
  • 甘蕉惊喜告生 - "甘蕉" is a proper name, and "惊喜" means "surprised and delighted." This phrase indicates that Ganniao was surprised and delighted and told the man.
  • 询之，以蛇酒告 - "询之" means "asked him," and "以蛇酒告" means "told him about snake wine." This phrase indicates that the man asked and was told about the snake wine.
  • 趋视，则遍体黑章成云篆，顶有独角，色殷然 - "趋视" means "rushed to see," and "黑章成云篆" means "black marks forming cloud-like patterns." "顶有独角" means "with a single horn on its head," and "色殷然" means "with a dark red color." This phrase describes the snake's appearance.
  • 盖此山蛇王，名乌风者也 - "盖" means "it is said that," and "乌风" is the name of the snake. This phrase identifies the snake as the king of the mountain snakes named Wufeng.
  • 具锦囊绮裙，花钿珠玉，妆女出见翁与诸宛若 - "具" means "prepared," and "锦囊绮裙" means "embroidered bags and silk skirts." "花钿珠玉" means "flower-shaped decorations and pearl jewelry." This phrase describes the woman being dressed in fine attire.
  • 莫不惊为天人 - "莫不" means "all," and "惊为天人" means "were astonished to think she was a heavenly being." This phrase indicates that everyone was amazed by her beauty.
  • 吾幼闻蛇王居此山千年矣 - "吾幼" means "when I was young," and "千年矣" means "for a thousand years." This phrase indicates that the old man had heard that the snake king had lived in the mountain for a thousand years.
  • 番僧求得片鳞，为人医癣疥，不可得 - "番僧" means "foreign monks," and "片鳞" means "a piece of scales." "医癣疥" means "to treat skin conditions like eczema or scabies." This phrase indicates that foreign monks had tried to obtain a piece of the snake's scales to treat skin conditions but failed.
  • 孰知天专留此，为吾疗贤妇疾耶 - "孰知" means "who would have thought," and "天专留此" means "that heaven had specially preserved this." "疗贤妇疾" means "to heal the disease of my virtuous wife." This phrase expresses the old man's gratitude to heaven for preserving the snake to heal his wife.
  • 即日备礼为合卺 - "即日" means "on the same day," and "备礼" means "prepared a ceremony." "合卺" refers to the wedding ceremony. This phrase indicates that the old man immediately prepared a wedding ceremony.
  • 珠履满堂，吹鼓筵宴，百里外男妇咸奔至，一觇女之颜色，归以为荣 - "珠履满堂" means "the hall was filled with people wearing pearl shoes," indicating a grand gathering. "吹鼓筵宴" means "music and feasting." "百里外男妇咸奔至" means "men and women from a hundred miles away rushed to attend." "一觇女之颜色" means "to catch a glimpse of the woman's beauty." "归以为荣" means "returned home feeling honored." This phrase describes the grand wedding celebration and the widespread admiration for the woman.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蛇毒 - Refers to the venom of a snake, which is often associated with powerful and sometimes dangerous effects in Chinese culture.
  • 鸩 - A type of bird whose liver was believed to be highly toxic and used as a poison. In this context, it is used as a metaphor for a potent substance.
  • 蛇酒 - A traditional Chinese remedy made by soaking snake parts in alcohol, often used for medicinal purposes.
  • 云篆 - Refers to cloud-like patterns, often associated with divine or mystical symbols in Chinese culture.
  • 锦囊绮裙，花钿珠玉 - Describes elaborate and luxurious clothing and accessories, typical of high-status individuals in Chinese culture.
  • 番僧 - Refers to foreign monks, often associated with Buddhism and the transmission of religious and medical knowledge.
  • 合卺 - A traditional Chinese wedding ceremony where the bride and groom share a cup of wine, symbolizing their union.
  • 珠履 - Refers to shoes adorned with pearls, indicating wealth and status.
  • 吹鼓筵宴 - Describes a grand feast accompanied by music, typical of important social events in Chinese culture.
  • 百里外男妇咸奔至 - Indicates the widespread social impact and importance of the event, reflecting the value placed on such occasions in Chinese society.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase "心憶蛇毒或可代鸩" is a metaphorical comparison, and the exact meaning of "代鸩" needs to be clarified.
  • The unit "升许" in the phrase "掬饮升许" is ambiguous and may need to be specified.
  • The sequence of actions in "心顿清醒，怯烦襟，肤转奇痒，又掬以洗涤，痒顿止" is linked by cause and effect, and the transitions need to be clearly rendered.
  • The repetition of "潜" in "明日，又潜饮而潜洗之" suggests a secretive and repeated action, which should be clearly conveyed.
  • The long sequence in "疾若失，肤之燥者，转莹如玉，发之卷者，转垂若云，面目手足之皴者，转如花如月，如嫩笋芽矣" describes a series of changes, and the transitions need to be clearly linked.
  • The sequence of events involving Ganniao and the man in "甘蕉惊喜告生，询之，以蛇酒告" should be clearly ordered.
  • The description of the snake's appearance in "趋视，则遍体黑章成云篆，顶有独角，色殷然" is detailed and should be rendered clearly.
  • The identification of the snake as the king of the mountain snakes in "盖此山蛇王，名乌风者也" should be clearly stated.
  • The description of the woman's attire and the context of her appearance in "具锦囊绮裙，花钿珠玉，妆女出见翁与诸宛若" should be clearly linked.
  • The reaction of the people to the woman's appearance in "莫不惊为天人" should be clearly conveyed.
  • The old man's recollection of the snake king's longevity in "吾幼闻蛇王居此山千年矣" should be clearly stated.
  • The failed attempts of foreign monks to obtain the snake's scales in "番僧求得片鳞，为人医癣疥，不可得" should be clearly described.
  • The old man's gratitude to heaven for preserving the snake in "孰知天专留此，为吾疗贤妇疾耶" should be clearly expressed.
  • The old man's immediate preparation of a wedding ceremony in "即日备礼为合卺" should be clearly indicated.
  • The grand wedding celebration and widespread admiration for the woman in "珠履满堂，吹鼓筵宴，百里外男妇咸奔至，一觇女之颜色，归以为荣" should be clearly described.

  • 女年甫四十有五，犹妍丽若天人 - Literally 'the daughter was just forty-five years old, still beautiful like a celestial being,' highlighting her beauty even at an older age.
  • 一夕，梦伟丈夫荷双龙来 - Literally 'one night, she dreamed of a great man carrying two dragons coming,' a vivid and symbolic dream.
  • 内皆纱帽累累，举而遍挂 - Literally 'inside were many hats, raised and hung everywhere,' describing the dream's imagery.
  • 屋角几满，尾更有缨帽衔宝石挂孔翠者亦夥 - Literally 'the corners of the house were almost full, and at the end, there were many hats with tassels, inlaid with gems, and hanging with peacock feathers,' further detailing the dream.
  • 丈夫挂讫，故俯瞰笼内，啧啧称有余 - Literally 'the man finished hanging, then looked down into the cage, murmuring that there was plenty,' suggesting a sense of abundance.
  • 寐告佟，知子孙必多有科甲显宦者 - Literally 'she told Tong in her sleep, knowing that her descendants would surely have many successful scholars and officials,' a prophecy based on the dream.
  • 然时方明季，不识缨帽是何威仪 - Literally 'but at that time, it was the late Ming dynasty, and no one knew what the tassels and hats represented,' indicating a lack of understanding of the dream's symbols.
  • 至盛朝，佟姓世居鲁，仍簪缨拾青紫 - Literally 'until the prosperous dynasty, the surname Tong lived in Lu, still wearing official hats and robes,' indicating continued prosperity and status.
  • 此桃源令孙梦麟所云 - Literally 'this is what the magistrate of Taoyuan, Sun Menglin, said,' attributing the story to a specific source.
  • 懊侬氏曰：彼美人兮，天涯海角，而氤氲簿上，早订婚姻，鬼神无奈何，始幻此缩地法耳 - Literally 'Aonge said: That beautiful woman, no matter where she is, and on the book of fate, marriage was already arranged, and the gods and spirits could do nothing, so they used this method of shrinking the earth,' a poetic and mystical interpretation.
  • 当不负死友，临奠数言，实诸天菩萨所共闻之，而一齐附泪者也 - Literally 'She would not let down her deceased friend, and when she spoke a few words at the funeral, it was truly heard by all the bodhisattvas, and they all shed tears together,' emphasizing the emotional weight of the words.
  • 或另注奇缘，使之得内助，享厚福，亦未可定 - Literally 'Perhaps another explanation for a strange fate, allowing her to get help from within, enjoy great fortune, is also possible,' suggesting alternative interpretations.
  • 尝闻四川周姓，亦曾有之，与此事同 - Literally 'I have heard that the Zhou family in Sichuan also had something similar, like this story,' indicating a parallel case.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 明季 - Refers to the late Ming dynasty, a period of political and social turmoil.
  • 缨帽 - Refers to official hats, often associated with high status and power.
  • 桃源令孙梦麟 - Refers to Sun Menglin, the magistrate of Taoyuan, a specific historical figure.
  • 诸天菩萨 - Refers to bodhisattvas, beings in Buddhist tradition who are enlightened but delay their own nirvana to help others.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 女仅生子一，因体羸多疾，为夫置妾媵，生子女若林立 - This sentence is a sequence of actions and conditions that must be read as a coherent narrative.
  • 郝夫人性惟钟爱女，环西苑，筑甥馆同居焉 - This clause describes the wife's preference and the family arrangement, which must be read together.
  • 阡陌云连，楼阁华伟，不啻世家 - This descriptive clause must be read as a single unit to capture the grandeur of the setting.
  • 至诸子成立，游庠序，女年甫四十有五，犹妍丽若天人 - This sequence of events and descriptions must be read as a continuous narrative.
  • 一夕，梦伟丈夫荷双龙来，内皆纱帽累累，举而遍挂，屋角几满，尾更有缨帽衔宝石挂孔翠者亦夥 - This long, vivid dream description must be read as a single, cohesive image.
  • 丈夫挂讫，故俯瞰笼内，啧啧称有余，戏掀掷过西墙，笑曰：‘便宜他。’ - This sequence of actions in the dream must be read as a continuous narrative.
  • 寐告佟，知子孙必多有科甲显宦者 - This clause must be read as a prophecy based on the dream.
  • 然时方明季，不识缨帽是何威仪 - This clause must be read as a commentary on the dream's symbols and their meaning at the time.
  • 至盛朝，佟姓世居鲁，仍簪缨拾青紫 - This clause must be read as a continuation of the family's prosperity and status.
  • 此桃源令孙梦麟所云 - This clause must be read as an attribution of the story to a specific source.
  • 懊侬氏曰：彼美人兮，天涯海角，而氤氲簿上，早订婚姻，鬼神无奈何，始幻此缩地法耳 - This clause must be read as a poetic and mystical interpretation of the story.
  • 当不负死友，临奠数言，实诸天菩萨所共闻之，而一齐附泪者也 - This clause must be read as an emotional and spiritual reflection on the story.
  • 或另注奇缘，使之得内助，享厚福，亦未可定 - This clause must be read as a suggestion of alternative interpretations.
  • 尝闻四川周姓，亦曾有之，与此事同 - This clause must be read as a parallel case to the main story.

Proper Nouns

  • 佟 - The main character or a key figure in the story.
  • 桃源令孙梦麟 - Sun Menglin, the magistrate of Taoyuan.
  • 诸天菩萨 - Bodhisattvas in Buddhist tradition.
  • 四川周姓 - The Zhou family in Sichuan, a parallel case mentioned in the story.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 众抚掌曰 - Common way to introduce a collective response in Classical Chinese.
  • 审是 - Conditional phrase used to confirm a statement, often seen in legal and narrative texts.
  • 博缠头锦 - Metaphor for winning a prize, often used in gambling contexts, reflecting the cultural significance of games and rewards.
  • 含笑 - Descriptive phrase indicating a cheerful or amused demeanor, often used to convey a character's emotional state.
  • 呼婢捧磁瓯出 - Detailed action involving a servant, reflecting the hierarchical and service-oriented nature of classical Chinese society.
  • 中列骰子六 - Six dice were arranged in the bowl, a common setup for dice games, reflecting the popularity of gambling in classical Chinese culture.
  • 供几中央 - Placed in the center of the table, indicating the importance of the object being placed.
  • 再拜安置讫 - Formal ritual action, reflecting the importance of rituals in classical Chinese society.
  • 妾名虽倚门，实则求偶 - Statement of dual identity, reflecting the complex social roles of women in classical Chinese society.
  • 请以相思之骨，代风月之媒 - Poetic way of saying she is seeking a genuine relationship, reflecting the literary and romantic traditions of the time.
  • 遵吾师命也 - Following a higher authority, reflecting the importance of hierarchy and obedience in classical Chinese society.
  • 来者以白金十两作孤注 - Gambling term, reflecting the cultural significance of games and betting in classical Chinese society.
  • 无论齿高下，貌妍媸 - Indicating that the criteria for the bet are not based on appearance, reflecting the social values of the time.
  • 一掷成六红者 - Referring to a specific outcome in dice rolling, reflecting the rules and mechanics of classical Chinese games.
  • 即嫁为妇 - Reward for winning the bet, reflecting the social and economic aspects of marriage in classical Chinese society.
  • 无食言 - Promise of honesty, reflecting the importance of trust and integrity in classical Chinese society.
  • 十两仅一掷 - Initial bet amount, reflecting the economic context of the time.
  • 再掷再破悭 - Rule for subsequent bets, reflecting the mechanics of gambling in classical Chinese society.
  • 妾守信 - Reinforcing her commitment, reflecting the importance of loyalty and trust in classical Chinese society.
  • 君等毋吝财也 - Friendly encouragement to bet, reflecting the social dynamics of the time.
  • 妙哉！ - Exclamation of approval, reflecting the cultural significance of praise and admiration.
  • 真别树艳帜，而另系赤绳者 - Compliment on the innovation, reflecting the cultural value of creativity and uniqueness.
  • 呼僮竞取白镪至 - Detailed action involving servants, reflecting the hierarchical and service-oriented nature of classical Chinese society.
  • 博竟日 - Gambling all day, reflecting the cultural significance of games and entertainment in classical Chinese society.
  • 皆色沮去 - All left dejected, reflecting the emotional impact of losing in games.
  • 远近哗噪 - Near and far, there was commotion, reflecting the widespread attention and interest in the event.
  • 何物粉儿 - Colloquial term for a courtesan, reflecting the social and cultural context of the time.
  • 独翻花样 - Uniquely innovative, reflecting the cultural value of creativity and originality.
  • 唱求牡之雉，呼守雌之卢 - Metaphor for seeking a mate, reflecting the cultural and literary traditions of the time.
  • 花骨头岂真作冰 - Metaphorical statement about the nature of the game, reflecting the cultural and literary traditions of the time.
  • 阿堵物依然入橐 - Silver still goes into the bag, indicating that despite the innovation, the game still involves money.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 明日又来，视瓯中已别五红 - Idiomatic phrase indicating the next day, and looking into the bowl to see five reds (likely referring to a game of chance).
  • 其一滚转不定 - Describes a ball rolling and not settling, indicating uncertainty in the game.
  • 公子大声呼红 - The young man loudly calls out for red, a common action in gambling games.
  • 比走盘珠定 - Metaphor comparing the movement of the ball to a pearl rolling in a dish, indicating it eventually settles.
  • 乃痴立如木鸡 - Vivid description of the young man standing motionless, as if turned to stone.
  • 女笑曰：“险哉！何一红之难也？” - The woman laughs and comments on the difficulty of getting one red, emphasizing the challenge.
  • 女貌虽极妍，而性极生硬 - Describes the woman as extremely beautiful but also very stubborn and hard-hearted.
  • 客至，略寒暄，即请博，博已，即下逐客令 - Describes the sequence of events when guests arrive: brief greetings, then asking to play, followed by being asked to leave immediately after the game.
  • 不许少一逗留 - Means not allowing any delay or lingering.
  • 公子恚怒，阴嗾无赖子三四辈，给金，使诣女室 - Describes the young man's anger and his plan to send a few ruffians to harass the woman.
  • 将窘辱，洗愧恧 - The goal of the ruffians is to embarrass and humiliate the woman, thereby washing away the young man's shame.
  • 女已审其意，待金入己橐，即挥手使去 - The woman understands the plan and, once she receives the money, dismisses the ruffians.
  • 无赖子大哄，曰：“一脔肉，竟如此昂贵耶？” - The ruffians complain that the woman is too expensive, using a metaphor for her value.
  • 彼博场尚有闲菜酒，为吾辈解饥渴 - They suggest that the gambling hall still has free food and drink to satisfy their hunger and thirst.
  • 言已，揽袖欲侮，女声色不动，略拂纤指，即纷跌庭外数十步 - The ruffians try to assault her, but she remains calm and with a slight flick of her fingers, they fall outside the courtyard.
  • 呼婢出夏楚，待其三蹶而三拍之，跪谢始释，鼠窜可怜 - The woman calls her maid to administer punishment, and the ruffians are hit three times, bow in apology, and then scurry away pitifully.
  • 后有踵至者，咸悉彼姝艳而勇，无敢亵，负者怏怏，不闻有后言 - Subsequent visitors learn of her beauty and courage and no longer dare to disrespect her. Those who lost are disgruntled but do not speak further.
  • 三载中，均落落小酬应，从不歌一声，陪一宴，假一颦笑，而香奁中累累盈五万金矣 - Over three years, she rarely entertains, never sings, attends banquets, or smiles, yet her wealth accumulates to a significant amount.
  • 偶语客曰：“曩得采仅娶吾身，今并得吾财，何乐而不为？” - She occasionally tells guests that in the past, winning only meant marrying her, but now it also means winning her wealth, making it even more appealing.
  • 于是门前车马，更喧如市井矣 - As a result, the front of her house becomes even more crowded with vehicles and people, like a bustling market.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 公子 - Refers to a young nobleman or gentleman, a common term in classical Chinese literature.
  • 瓯 - A type of bowl or dish, often used in gambling games.
  • 红 - Refers to a specific color or symbol in gambling, often associated with winning.
  • 无赖子 - Ruffians or troublemakers, often used to describe lower-class individuals who engage in mischief.
  • 夏楚 - A type of punishment, often involving physical discipline.
  • 香奁 - A box for storing valuables, often used to symbolize wealth or personal possessions.

Proper Nouns

  • 无 - A common term for a ruffian or troublemaker.
  • 夏楚 - A type of punishment, often involving physical discipline.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 避難走燕台 - Fleeing to Yan Platform to avoid trouble; *燕台* refers to a place of refuge or a strategic location.
  • 落拓殊甚 - Extremely destitute and aimless; *落拓* describes a state of being penniless and without prospects.
  • 守囊之銀，僅僅十兩 - The silver in his bag, just ten taels; *守囊之銀* refers to the small amount of money he has.
  • 計無出 - No plan could be devised; indicates a state of helplessness.
  • 謀於同鄉某 - Consulted with a certain fellow villager; *謀* means to consult or plan.
  • 子知此間有雪里紅乎？ - Do you know if there is a Snow Red here? *雪里紅* is likely a metaphorical or colloquial term, possibly referring to a gambling game or a person.
  • 拼與一戰 - Wager a battle; *拼* implies risking everything in a single effort.
  • 捷則立地成富耳 - If victorious, instantly become wealthy; *立地* emphasizes the immediacy of the result.
  • 負則尋女未遲也 - If defeated, it’s not too late to seek a woman; *尋女* could mean seeking a partner or a woman’s favor.
  • 卿亦過小觑人矣 - You underestimate me too much; *小觑* means to look down upon or underestimate.
  • 書生雖貧，量不致為些須露乞兒態 - Though poor, a scholar would not stoop to the beggarly demeanor of a trifling matter; *些須* means trivial, and *乞兒態* refers to the behavior of a beggar.
  • 言辭慷慨，其氣熊熊 - His words were resolute, and his spirit was fiery; *熊熊* conveys a sense of intense passion or energy.
  • 階下應 - There was a response from below the stairs; *階下* refers to the area below the stairs, possibly a servant or a hidden observer.
  • 燃兩畫燭如椽 - Lit two painted candles as thick as pillars; *畫燭* refers to elaborately decorated candles, and *如椽* compares their thickness to a pillar.
  • 水陸新進 - New arrivals from both land and sea; *水陸* refers to both terrestrial and maritime sources.
  • 婢子引生入曲室 - The maid led him into a secluded room; *曲室* refers to a private or hidden room.
  • 沐浴更新衣 - Bathed and changed into new clothes; *沐浴* means to bathe, and *更新衣* means to put on new clothes.
  • 歸洞房 - Returned to the bridal chamber; *洞房* is a term for the newlywed bedroom.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然为贾则素恶铜臭，为儒则不耐毡寒 - A contrastive expression meaning 'if I were to become a merchant, I would always dislike the smell of money; if I were to become a scholar, I would not tolerate the cold of the felt robes.'
  • 无已，其一行作吏，尚可以身许国乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'If not, perhaps I should serve as an official and still be able to dedicate myself to the state.'
  • 妾尚女儿身，唯夫君怜之 - A humble and affectionate expression from the woman, meaning 'I am still a maiden, only my husband can pity me.'
  • 皮相者，疑卿为章台柳；福艳者，知卿是佛池莲 - A metaphorical expression comparing the woman's appearance to a willow tree on Changtai Street (a famous street for courtesans) and her inner beauty to a lotus flower in Buddha's pool.
  • 仆心醉矣，且爱且怜 - An expression of deep admiration and affection, meaning 'I am utterly captivated, loving and cherishing you.'
  • 明晨，视红褪如洗 - A vivid description meaning 'The next morning, I saw the red flush fade away as if washed clean.'
  • 旋以片纸为生纳粟，得铨闽之某县令事 - A bureaucratic expression meaning 'She immediately used a small piece of paper to help me register for a position as a county magistrate in Fujian.'
  • 女不令多随从，卷厚资，与婢妪均易男子装，己则缨曼胡，吉莫靴，负弹弓，佩长剑，四骑登程 - A detailed description of the woman's preparation for travel, meaning 'She did not allow many attendants, gathered a large sum of money, and had her maids and servants dress in men's clothing. She herself wore a silk scarf, a Manchu hat, and leather boots, carried a slingshot, and wore a long sword. They set off on four horses.'
  • 猝遇响马贼呼啸至 - A dramatic expression meaning 'Suddenly encountered a gang of bandits who came charging.'
  • 生战栗，面灰死 - A vivid description of fear, meaning 'I trembled with fear, my face turning pale as if dead.'
  • 女命婢妪护郎君，己则纵辔迓贼锋，发连珠铁弹子，如秋风摧落叶，贼披靡，无生还者 - A detailed description of the woman's actions, meaning 'She ordered her maids and servants to protect the young master, while she herself galloped forward to meet the bandits, firing a continuous stream of iron bullets like autumn wind tearing leaves, causing the bandits to scatter, none of them surviving.'
  • 生大骇，几坠骑 - A vivid description of shock, meaning 'I was greatly frightened and nearly fell off my horse.'
  • 至前站，私询其何勇过健儿 - A question about the woman's bravery, meaning 'When we reached the next station, I privately asked her how she could be braver than a strong man.'
  • 女笑曰：“措大尚藐视巾帼耶？聂隐、红线辈，自在人间，惜肉眼人不识耳” - A humorous and confident response, meaning 'She laughed and said, 'How can you still look down on women? Heroes like Nie Ying and Red Line are still alive in the world; it's just that ordinary people don't recognize them.''
  • 因把酒，重述都中拍无赖事，相与捧腹 - A description of a joyful moment, meaning 'She then offered me wine, retold stories of her exploits in the capital, and we both laughed heartily.'
  • 到闽谒长吏，女均执刺代长须，致无陨越 - A description of the woman's actions upon arriving in Fujian, meaning 'When we arrived in Fujian and met with local officials, she took charge, representing me as if I were a seasoned official, ensuring that nothing went wrong.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贾 - Merchant, referring to a businessman.
  • 儒 - Scholar, referring to a person of learning and Confucian values.
  • 铜臭 - The smell of money, often used to criticize materialism.
  • 毡寒 - The cold of felt robes, a reference to the discomfort of wearing traditional scholar's clothing.
  • 章台柳 - A famous street in Chang'an known for its courtesans, symbolizing beauty and seduction.
  • 佛池莲 - A lotus flower in Buddha's pool, symbolizing purity and inner beauty.
  • 响马贼 - A type of bandit in Chinese history, often associated with horse thieves.
  • 聂隐 - Legendary female hero in Chinese folklore, known for her bravery and skill.
  • 红线 - Legendary female hero in Chinese folklore, known for her bravery and skill.
  • 闽 - Fujian Province, a region in southeastern China.
  • 县令 - County magistrate, a local government official.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夤僚瞰艳仆 - Describes a situation where someone is suspicious of another's behavior, possibly due to an unusual or secretive action.
  • 疑其有断袖癖 - An idiom referring to a suspicion of homosexuality, based on the ancient story of Emperor Ai of Han and Dong Xian.
  • 实不知即使君采苹妇也 - Indicates that the person being suspected is actually married to a woman, not a man.
  • 抵任不半载 - Means 'not even half a year after taking office.'
  • 豕突至闽 - A metaphor for the enemy's rapid and destructive advance, like wild boars rushing into a place.
  • 蓦围斗大城 - Describes the enemy suddenly surrounding a small but significant city.
  • 生集乡团 - Means 'the official gathered local militia.'
  • 女帘后窥之 - Means 'the woman watched from behind the curtain.'
  • 若以此御贼，直摧枯拉朽耳 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'if you use this method to fight the enemy, it will be as easy as breaking down rotten wood and crumbling walls.'
  • 寇深矣，且奈何 - Means 'the enemy is deep inside, what shall we do now?'
  • 翌日，贼更集 - Means 'the next day, the enemy gathered again.'
  • 肆猱升 - Describes the enemy climbing up the walls like monkeys.
  • 将瓦解 - Means 'about to collapse.'
  • 女麾众登陴 - Means 'the woman led the people to climb the city walls.'
  • 斫杀一昼夜 - Means 'they fought and killed for a whole day and night.'
  • 攻始懈 - Means 'the attack finally slackened.'
  • 然耽耽者犹不去 - Means 'but the persistent ones still did not leave.'
  • 生乞救于中丞 - Means 'the official begged for help from the Governor-General.'
  • 牍三申而援不至 - Means 'three times he sent messages for help, but no aid arrived.'
  • 盖驿路断，恨无神邮也 - Means 'because the postal route was cut off, it was regrettable that there was no divine messenger.'
  • 至夕，女以大爵劝生饮 - Means 'by evening, the woman offered the official a large cup of wine to drink.'
  • 生忧惧，涓滴难下咽 - Means 'the official, full of worry and fear, could hardly swallow even a drop.'
  • 饮亦死，不饮亦死，曷为醉觞乎 - Means 'whether you drink or not, you will die, so why not get drunk?'
  • 生始鲸吸数十爵 - Means 'the official then drank several large cups in one gulp.'
  • 沉酣眠所坐木椅上 - Means 'he fell into a deep sleep on the wooden chair he was sitting on.'
  • 女出，裂帛长十数丈者 - Means 'the woman left, took out a long piece of silk cloth.'
  • 先缚生，然后荷于背 - Means 'first she bound the official, then carried him on her back.'
  • 且缚之极坚固，若负襁褓儿 - Means 'she bound him very tightly, as if carrying a baby in a swaddling cloth.'
  • 仍男装，右提剑，左握护身牌 - Means 'she dressed in men's clothes, holding a sword in her right hand and a protective shield in her left.'
  • 听野鸡三唱，晨月朦胧 - Means 'she waited until the roosters crowed three times, and the morning moon was dim.'
  • 呼乡团而告之曰 - Means 'she called out to the local militia and said to them.'
  • 事急矣，吾负汝使君往乞援，三日即返 - Means 'the situation is urgent, I will carry your governor to seek help, and I will return in three days.'
  • 城中诸事，听吾婢妪指挥，可谧无恐也 - Means 'all matters in the city will be under the command of my maids and elderly women, you can rest assured.'
  • 遂开关，放之出 - Means 'she then opened the gate and let them out.'
  • 贼瞰其越濠，行甚速 - Means 'the enemy saw them crossing the moat, moving very quickly.'
  • 又见所负如屋上瓴，不识云何 - Means 'they also saw what she was carrying as if it were a water pot on the roof, not knowing what it was.'
  • 始痴望，继环击 - Means 'they first stared in amazement, then surrounded and attacked.'
  • 女且走且御，斩数十人而出重围焉 - Means 'the woman fought while running, cutting down dozens of enemies and escaping the encirclement.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 断袖癖 - A reference to the ancient Chinese story of Emperor Ai of Han and Dong Xian, where the emperor cut his sleeve to avoid disturbing Dong Xian while sleeping.
  • 采苹妇 - Refers to a married woman, specifically the wife of the official.
  • 中丞 - Refers to the Governor-General, a high-ranking official.
  • 神邮 - A metaphor for a swift and reliable messenger, often associated with divine intervention.
  • 野鸡三唱 - A reference to the rooster crowing three times, indicating the start of the day.
  • 晨月朦胧 - Describes the dim light of the morning moon, a common poetic image in Chinese literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 金陵 - The ancient name for Nanjing, a city in China.
  • 中丞 - The title of the Governor-General, a high-ranking official.
  • 野鸡 - The rooster, a common symbol in Chinese literature.
  • 晨月 - The morning moon, a common poetic image in Chinese literature.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 縣距省垣三百里 - Literally means 'the county is 300 li away from the provincial capital.' Requires context to understand the distance in modern terms (1 li ≈ 0.5 km).
  • 甫卓午 - Literally means 'just at noon.' Indicates the exact time.
  • 解背上良人 - Literally means 'to take off the husband's back.' Metaphorical expression indicating the removal of a burden or the act of carrying something.
  • 殘醉猶未醒 - Literally means 'the lingering drunkenness has not yet cleared.' Describes someone who is still slightly intoxicated.
  • 唯諾 - Literally means 'to agree and nod.' Formal way of assenting to a request or command.
  • 屬吏自检择 - Literally means 'the subordinate officials will select themselves.' Implies a self-selection process.
  • 捷如猿猱 - Literally means 'as agile as a monkey.' Simile describing speed and agility.
  • 勇如罴虎 - Literally means 'as brave as a bear and a tiger.' Simile describing great courage.
  • 山荆 - A humble term used by women to refer to themselves, similar to 'your humble servant.'
  • 明府 - A respectful term for a local official, often used by subordinates.
  • 鄭樱桃 - A name, possibly a nickname or a specific person.
  • 圍解日 - Literally means 'the day of the siege's resolution.' Refers to the end of a military operation.
  • 凱旋 - Literally means 'victorious return.' Describes a triumphant return after a successful military campaign.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 縣 - Refers to a county, a local administrative division.
  • 省垣 - Refers to the provincial capital or the central government.
  • 中丞 - A high-ranking official, often a provincial governor or a deputy governor.
  • 抚军 - A military governor or a high-ranking military official.
  • 麾下 - Literally means 'under the banner,' referring to the troops or subordinates under a commander.
  • 演武场 - A place for military training and drills.
  • 雉堞 - Refers to the battlements or walls of a fortress.
  • 馘 - Refers to the ears or heads of defeated enemies, often taken as trophies.
  • 孽 - Refers to evil or wicked people, often used in the context of rebels or bandits.

Proper Nouns

  • 中丞 - A high-ranking official.
  • 抚军 - A military governor.
  • 演武场 - A specific location for military training.
  • 雉堞 - The walls of a fortress.
  • 馘 - A term for the ears or heads of defeated enemies.
  • 孽 - A term for evil or wicked people.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卓异 - Adjective meaning 'outstanding' or 'excellent,' used to describe the official's exceptional performance.
  • 迁郡守 - Term indicating promotion to the position of magistrate of a prefecture.
  • 女诏封成夫人 - Phrase indicating that the emperor granted her the title of 'Lady Cheng.'
  • 罗绮中人 - Metaphor for women of high social status, particularly those in the imperial harem or noble families.
  • 八阵 - Refers to the Eight Formation Diagrams, a concept from the *I Ching* (Book of Changes) used here metaphorically to describe strategic thinking.
  • 目无全牛 - Metaphor from the *Zhuangzi* meaning 'seeing through the whole ox,' indicating mastery and expertise.
  • 桃花门巷 - Metaphor for brothels or places of entertainment, often associated with the Song Dynasty.
  • 锦绣城 - Metaphor for a prosperous and refined city, often used to describe a place of beauty and wealth.
  • 雪里红 - A type of Chinese cabbage, used here metaphorically to symbolize something that is both humble and valuable.
  • 成夫人菜 - Metaphorical name for the type of cabbage, derived from the story of Lady Cheng.
  • 诸葛行营之种 - Metaphorical reference to the legacy of Zhuge Liang, a famous military strategist, indicating that the cabbage has a noble and enduring lineage.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 成夫人 - Title given to a woman who is granted a noble status by the emperor, often due to her family's service or influence.
  • 桃李之艳，而姜桂之性 - Metaphor combining the beauty of peaches and plums (symbolizing grace and elegance) with the spiciness of ginger and cinnamon (symbolizing strength and courage).
  • 闽之氓 - Refers to the people of Fujian province, known for their agricultural practices and respect for tradition.
  • 伏腊 - Traditional Chinese festivals, specifically the Double Ninth Festival (Chongyang Festival) and the Winter Solstice Festival.
  • 春盘 - A traditional Chinese dish served during the Spring Festival, often containing various vegetables and fruits.
  • 诸葛行营之种 - Reference to Zhuge Liang, a renowned military strategist and statesman of the Three Kingdoms period, known for his strategic acumen and loyalty.

  • 秦鲁臣 - A historian, the source of the report.
  • 金陵 - The ancient name for Nanjing, a city in Jiangsu province.
  • 闽 - The abbreviation for Fujian province.

  • 雪里红 - A type of Chinese cabbage.
  • 成夫人菜 - Metaphorical name for the type of cabbage.
  • 诸葛行营之种 - Metaphorical reference to the legacy of Zhuge Liang.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 未婚 - Refers to the state of being unmarried, a common term in classical texts.
  • 移枕席 - Literally 'move the pillow and mat,' a euphemism for moving to a different room or setting.
  • 岑寂 - Describes a quiet, secluded, and peaceful environment.
  • 溽暑 - Refers to the oppressive heat and humidity typical of summer.
  • 思 - Here it means 'to think' or 'to reflect,' often in a contemplative sense.
  • 曼声吟咏 - Describes singing or reciting in a prolonged, melodious voice.
  • 绮户 - Refers to an ornate or beautifully decorated door or window.
  • 解结之锥 - A metaphorical expression where '解结' means 'to untie a knot,' and '锥' is a sharp-pointed tool, suggesting something that can easily penetrate or resolve an issue.
  • 绮户为谁开 - A rhetorical question asking who would open such a beautiful door or window, implying loneliness or solitude.
  • 卿 - A respectful term for 'you,' often used in classical Chinese.
  • 鬼耶 - A question asking if someone is a ghost.
  • 人耶 - A question asking if someone is a human.
  • 狐耶 - A question asking if someone is a fox, often associated with fox spirits in Chinese folklore.
  • 情郎 - Refers to a lover or sweetheart.
  • 冒嫌 - Means 'to take a risk' or 'to face suspicion.'
  • 红拂 - Refers to a famous courtesan and lover of the Tang dynasty general Li Shimin, known for her bold and romantic actions.
  • 名乎 - A question asking if someone has a name.
  • 字乎 - A question asking if someone has a courtesy name.
  • 宾奴 - A name given to the woman, possibly indicating her status or role.
  • 樊稚 - A courtesy name given to the woman.
  • 纤纤手 - Describes small, delicate hands.
  • 柔胜于荑 - Means 'softer than the finest grass,' emphasizing the woman's gentle touch.
  • 魂荡 - Describes a state of being deeply moved or entranced.

  • 任城 - Refers to Rencang, a place in Shandong Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 相与谈论 - To converse together, a common expression for engaging in dialogue.
  • 慧舌生香 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'the words are so wise and eloquent that they seem to emit a fragrance.'
  • 藻思耀采 - The thoughts are so rich and the expressions are so brilliant, describing the beauty and depth of the conversation.
  • 邬爱且服 - Wu loves and admires, indicating a strong emotional and intellectual connection.
  • 听玉漏丁丁 - Listening to the sound of the jade drip (a type of water clock), a poetic way to describe the passage of time.
  • 墙外之柝四下 - The watchman's drum beats four times outside the wall, indicating the time of night.
  • 促其解衣 - Urge him to take off his clothes, a polite way to suggest intimacy.
  • 飞红上颊 - Blush rises to the cheeks, a vivid description of embarrassment or excitement.
  • 约以明宵 - Agree to meet again the next night, a promise for a future encounter.
  • 野鸡四啼 - The roosters crow four times, another way to indicate the time of night.
  • 仓皇遽遁 - Flee in haste, describing a hurried departure.
  • 翌果挑灯自携衾枕至 - The next day, she indeed brings a lamp and her bedding, indicating preparation for intimacy.
  • 备极华丽 - Extremely luxurious, describing the opulence of the bedding.
  • 真犹处子 - Truly like a virgin, emphasizing the purity of the encounter.
  • 臂代枕 - Using her arm as a pillow, a romantic gesture.
  • 口吟一词 - Reciting a poem, a form of intimate expression.
  • 郎可怜，妾可怜 - Dear, I pity you; Dear, I pity me, expressing mutual affection and concern.
  • 一对鸳鸯一对鹣 - A pair of mandarin ducks and a pair of jia, a metaphor for a loving couple.
  • 今宵那世缘 - Tonight, our worldly缘分 (fate), expressing the significance of the moment.
  • 莫流连 - Do not linger, a warning to avoid excessive attachment.
  • 且流连 - Yet linger, a contradictory instruction that adds complexity to the advice.
  • 生怕钟鸣欲曙天 - Afraid that the bell will ring and dawn will break, expressing fear of the end of the night.
  • 情人隔一边 - Lovers separated by one side, a poignant expression of distance.
  • 风谁家，月谁家 - Whose is the wind? Whose is the moon? a rhetorical question expressing the universality of love.
  • 妾岂当门卖笑娃 - I am not a courtesan selling smiles at the door, emphasizing the sincerity of her feelings.
  • 情深念转差 - Deep feelings turn into thoughts of difference, expressing the complexity of emotions.
  • 香辟邪，正辟邪 - Pungent to ward off evil, correct to ward off evil, a metaphor for the protective power of love.
  • 夜雨摧残一树花 - Night rain damages a tree of flowers, a metaphor for the fragility of love.
  • 郎君郑重些 - Dear, be serious, a request for respect and care.
  • 自摘耳上两金环 - She herself picks off the two gold rings from her ears, a gesture of commitment.
  • 以此作定情物 - Use this as a token of love, a formal way to establish a relationship.
  • 慎勿示人 - Be careful not to show it to others, a warning to keep the relationship private.
  • 恐飞短流长 - Fear that rumors will spread, expressing concern about gossip.
  • 彼此不利 - Harm both of us, indicating the potential consequences of gossip.
  • 自此来无虚夕 - From then on, there was no empty night, indicating a regular pattern of meetings.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一夜正偎拥 - Literally means 'one night, they were hugging each other closely,' describing an intimate moment.
  • 斑白叟 - Refers to an elderly man with white hair, often used to denote a wise or authoritative figure.
  • 面靛裂，发蓬飞 - Describes a man with a disheveled appearance, his face distorted with rage, and his hair flying wildly.
  • 霜髯如戟 - A vivid metaphor comparing the old man's beard to spears, emphasizing his fierce and intimidating presence.
  • 小妮子，太不识羞耻 - A harsh rebuke, with '小妮子' referring to a young woman in a derogatory manner and '不识羞耻' meaning 'lacking shame.'
  • 污人清白 - Means 'to tarnish someone's purity or innocence.'
  • 风狂儿不当杀却耶 - A rhetorical question expressing the old man's anger, with '风狂儿' referring to the young man and '杀却' meaning 'to kill.'
  • 惊惶无地 - Means 'extremely frightened and at a loss.'
  • 口噤不能言 - Describes being unable to speak, with '口噤' meaning 'mute' or 'speechless.'
  • 齿牙震击作奇响 - Means 'the teeth and jaw clattering in a strange sound,' indicating extreme fear or shock.

  • 老父也 - Means 'my father,' revealing the identity of the old man.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 斑白叟 - In classical Chinese literature, an elderly man with white hair often symbolizes wisdom, authority, or even supernatural power.

  • 默无一言 - A phrase describing someone who is silent and without a word, often used to depict a character's state of mind in moments of shock or confusion.

  • 老叟属卿何人 - A question asking who the old man is related to, often used to reveal a character's identity or relationship.

  • 非他，骆氏小素也 - Means 'it is not someone else, it is Xiaosu of the Luo family', identifying the fox by name.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐 - Refers to a fox, often associated with supernatural beings in Chinese folklore, particularly fox spirits (huli jing) known for their beauty and seductive powers.

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 数日来报 - Formal expression meaning 'reports came after several days.'
  • 小素匆促，不及裁笺 - Literally 'Xiaosu was in a hurry and did not have time to write a letter.' Common way to express inability to write a formal letter due to time constraints.
  • 着传语奉复郎君 - Literally 'instructing to convey a message to the young master,' indicating message conveyed through a third party.
  • 前实缘尽 - Literally 'the previous connection was indeed severed,' suggesting end of a relationship or bond.
  • 恐径自别去 - Literally 'fearing that she would leave directly,' implying fear of abrupt separation.
  • 苦郎相思 - Literally 'making the young master suffer from longing,' indicating emotional distress.
  • 幻此形状 - Literally 'creating this form,' suggesting supernatural or magical transformation.
  • 俾郎君心死 - Literally 'making the young master's heart die,' implying desire to end young master's feelings of love.
  • 乘便寄丹砂一粒 - Literally 'taking the opportunity to send a piece of cinnabar,' indicating small gift sent along with message.
  • 可以却病痛 - Literally 'can dispel illness and pain,' suggesting medicinal properties of cinnabar.
  • 邬视药小而红，香甚 - Literally 'Wu saw the medicine small and red, very fragrant,' describing appearance and scent of cinnabar.
  • 一服，疾果瘳 - Literally 'after taking it once, the illness indeed disappeared,' indicating effectiveness of medicine.
  • 而思女之心亦释 - Literally 'and the heart's longing for the woman was also released,' indicating release of emotional distress.
  • 懊侬氏曰 - Formal way of introducing a commentator or author, similar to 'the author says' or 'the commentator remarks.'
  • 情之所在 - Literally 'where emotion lies,' emphasizing power and influence of emotion.
  • 父母师保不能止 - Literally 'parents, teachers, and guardians cannot stop it,' indicating irresistible nature of emotion.
  • 天地鬼神不能禁 - Literally 'heaven and earth, gods and spirits cannot forbid it,' further emphasizing overwhelming power of emotion.
  • 山川河海不能隔 - Literally 'mountains, rivers, seas, and oceans cannot separate it,' indicating boundless reach of emotion.
  • 顾为情而来，情未尽，则麾之不去 - Literally 'because of emotion, if the emotion is not exhausted, it cannot be dismissed,' suggesting true emotion persists until fully resolved.
  • 情既尽，即招之不来 - Literally 'if the emotion is exhausted, it cannot be called back,' indicating once emotion is resolved, it cannot be rekindled.
  • 不以余情害情人 - Literally 'not harming the beloved with leftover emotion,' suggesting selfless approach to love.
  • 复能以幻相警痴子 - Literally 'and can use a magical form to warn the foolish,' indicating supernatural intervention to protect beloved.
  • 是非真深于情者乎 - Literally 'is this not truly deep in emotion?' questioning depth and sincerity of emotion.
  • 否则，朝伐夕戕，非髓竭神枯而不已 - Literally 'otherwise, morning attacks and evening injuries, not stopping until the marrow is exhausted and the spirit is dried up,' indicating destructive nature of unrequited love.
  • 又或洁身而退，令人魂销气结不能忘，真冤且孽矣 - Literally 'or else, withdrawing oneself, making others' souls and spirits break and unable to forget, truly a grudge and a curse,' indicating lasting impact of unrequited love.
  • 尚得谓之情乎 - Literally 'can this still be called emotion?' questioning validity of such intense but ultimately unfulfilled emotion.
  • 如小素者，方可与言情 - Literally 'like Xiaosu, one can speak of emotion,' suggesting only those who understand true nature of emotion can truly discuss it.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 太守 - Refers to the governor, a high-ranking official in ancient China.
  • 苏台 - Refers to Suzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province, known for its cultural and economic significance.
  • 墨败 - Refers to a corruption scandal, where the governor is involved in bribery or other illegal activities.
  • 第一妾 - Refers to the first concubine, a secondary wife in the household hierarchy.
  • 豫人 - Refers to someone from Henan Province, indicating the friend's origin.
  • 古癖 - Refers to an old-fashioned or antiquarian habit, suggesting a love for ancient artifacts and cultural relics.
  • 烂铜碎玉，书画玩好 - Refers to the collection of broken copper, shattered jade, paintings, calligraphy, and other antiquities, reflecting the friend's interests.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence structure is somewhat elliptical, where '佳' takes responsibility for the governor's mistake and questions the governor's behavior.
  • This sentence structure is a direct quote, where the governor praises others in a flattering manner and expresses regret for not recognizing '万生' earlier.
  • This sentence structure is a sequence of actions, where the governor gives money and asks for a beautiful woman, presenting her as a local specialty.
  • This sentence structure describes a series of events, where the person buys a poor family's daughter and hesitates before proceeding.
  • This sentence structure links the corruption scandal to the person taking the poor family's daughter as his first concubine.
  • This sentence structure introduces the friend and his collection of antiquities.
  • This sentence structure describes the person's actions in searching for and collecting rare items, secretly keeping them and reporting only half the price.

Proper Nouns

  • 守 - The governor, a high-ranking official.
  • 佳 - A person who takes responsibility for the governor's mistake.
  • 万生 - A person whom the governor regrets not recognizing earlier.
  • 苏台 - Suzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province.
  • 春花 - The name of the poor family's daughter, who becomes the person's first concubine.
  • 豫人王七 - The person's friend from Henan Province, known for his antiquarian habits.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以故 - Indicates a cause or reason.
  • 破瓜年 - Idiom referring to a girl reaching the age of marriage, typically around 16.
  • 弱息 - Term of endearment for a daughter.
  • 仆 - Humble self-reference.
  • 却扇筵开 - Traditional custom where a bride removes her fan to reveal her face.
  • 掀帕斜睨 - Traditional gesture indicating a bride's readiness for marriage.
  • 目灼灼者，佳也 - Describes someone with bright, lively eyes.
  • 吾故疑是佳儿 - Suspected it was Jia's son; '故' means 'therefore' or 'so.'
  • 既有佳儿，理配佳妇 - Reasonable to marry Jia's son to a good wife; '理' means 'reason' or 'logic.'
  • 遂攘为己之第二妾 - Took her as his second concubine; '攘' means to seize or take.
  • 名曰夏云 - Named her Xiacun; '名曰' means 'named as.'
  • 偶乘焦叟船 - Traveled by Jiao Lao's boat; '偶' means 'once' or 'by chance.'
  • 过鄱阳 - Passed through Poyang; '鄱阳' is a place name.
  • 佳善酿，精烹调 - Jia was skilled in brewing and cooking; '善酿' means 'skilled in brewing,' and '精烹调' means 'excellent in cooking.'
  • 每餐必拉焦共啖酌 - Invited Jiao to eat and drink together at every meal; '必' means 'always' or 'necessarily.'
  • 欲迁东道，必坚止 - Firmly stopped him if he wanted to move to the east; '坚止' means 'firmly stop.'
  • 翁私带小货，佳辄浑其税而倍其值 - Covered the taxes and doubled the price when the old man secretly brought small goods; '辄' means 'always' or 'immediately.'
  • 瞰翁女，船尾操楫，甚艳冶 - Found the old man's daughter very beautiful and charming as she rowed at the stern of the boat; '瞰' means 'looked at,' and '艳冶' means 'beautiful and charming.'
  • 眉目传情 - Conveyed affection with her eyes and eyebrows; '传情' means 'conveyed affection.'
  • 何女公子至今无佳婿 - Asked why the daughter had not found a good husband yet; '女公子' means 'daughter,' and '佳婿' means 'good husband.'
  • 小妮子，娇惰惯，不欲嫁弄潮儿 - Described the little girl as spoiled and lazy, not wanting to marry a boatman; '小妮子' means 'little girl,' '娇惰' means 'spoiled and lazy,' and '弄潮儿' means 'boatman.'
  • 至瞿塘贾，金马客，仆又难仰攀 - Cannot aspire to the merchants of Qutang and the guests of Jinma; '瞿塘' and '金马' are place names, and '难仰攀' means 'cannot aspire to.'
  • 是以迟耳 - That is why it has been delayed; '是以' means 'that is why,' and '迟' means 'delayed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 王大信任 - Wang Daren trusted him greatly; Wang Daren is a historical figure known for his trustworthiness.
  • 破瓜年 - Marriageable age; traditional Chinese idiom referring to a girl's age of 16.
  • 弱息 - Daughter; term of endearment for a daughter.
  • 仆 - I; humble self-reference.
  • 却扇筵开 - The fan was removed, and the banquet began; traditional custom where a bride removes her fan to reveal her face.
  • 掀帕斜睨 - Lifted her veil and glanced sideways; traditional gesture indicating a bride's readiness for marriage.
  • 目灼灼者，佳也 - The one with bright eyes is Jia; describes someone with bright, lively eyes.
  • 吾故疑是佳儿 - Suspected it was Jia's son; '故' means 'therefore' or 'so.'
  • 既有佳儿，理配佳妇 - Since we have Jia's son, it is reasonable to marry him to a good wife; '理' means 'reason' or 'logic.'
  • 遂攘为己之第二妾 - He then took her as his second concubine; '攘' means to seize or take.
  • 名曰夏云 - He named her Xiacun; '名曰' means 'named as.'
  • 偶乘焦叟船 - Once, he traveled by Jiao Lao's boat; '偶' means 'once' or 'by chance.'
  • 过鄱阳 - Passed through Poyang; '鄱阳' is a place name.
  • 佳善酿，精烹调 - Jia was skilled in brewing and cooking; '善酿' means 'skilled in brewing,' and '精烹调' means 'excellent in cooking.'
  • 每餐必拉焦共啖酌 - At every meal, he would invite Jiao to eat and drink together; '必' means 'always' or 'necessarily.'
  • 欲迁东道，必坚止 - If he wanted to move to the east, Jiao would firmly stop him; '坚止' means 'firmly stop.'
  • 翁私带小货，佳辄浑其税而倍其值 - When the old man secretly brought small goods, Jia would cover the taxes and double the price; '辄' means 'always' or 'immediately.'
  • 瞰翁女，船尾操楫，甚艳冶 - He looked at the old man's daughter, who was rowing at the stern of the boat, and found her very beautiful and charming; '瞰' means 'looked at,' and '艳冶' means 'beautiful and charming.'
  • 眉目传情 - She conveyed affection with her eyes and eyebrows; '传情' means 'conveyed affection.'
  • 何女公子至今无佳婿 - Why has your daughter not found a good husband yet? '女公子' means 'daughter,' and '佳婿' means 'good husband.'
  • 小妮子，娇惰惯，不欲嫁弄潮儿 - That little girl is spoiled and lazy, and does not want to marry a boatman; '小妮子' means 'little girl,' '娇惰' means 'spoiled and lazy,' and '弄潮儿' means 'boatman.'
  • 至瞿塘贾，金马客，仆又难仰攀 - As for the merchants of Qutang and the guests of Jinma, I cannot aspire to them; '瞿塘' and '金马' are place names, and '难仰攀' means 'cannot aspire to.'
  • 是以迟耳 - That is why it has been delayed; '是以' means 'that is why,' and '迟' means 'delayed.'
